text_summary: BRAF V600E is the most common activating BRAF mutation, present in approximately 50% of melanomas, 10% of colorectal cancers, and 2-4% of NSCLCs. This missense variant results in constitutive activation of the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway, driving uncontrolled cell proliferation. FDA-approved targeted therapies include the BRAF inhibitors vemurafenib and dabrafenib, with combination BRAF+MEK inhibition (dabrafenib plus trametinib, encorafenib plus binimetinib) now the standard of care. In melanoma, BRAF V600E is CIViC evidence level A with validated clinical significance for predicting response to BRAF-targeted therapy. Resistance commonly develops through NRAS mutations, BRAF amplification, or MEK1/2 mutations within 6-12 months. In colorectal cancer, BRAF V600E requires triplet therapy with BRAF+MEK+EGFR inhibition due to feedback reactivation of EGFR signaling.
drugs: vemurafenib, dabrafenib, dabrafenib + trametinib, encorafenib + binimetinib
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to targeted therapy

text_summary: EGFR L858R is a recurrent activating point mutation in exon 21 of the epidermal growth factor receptor gene, found in approximately 40% of all EGFR-mutant NSCLCs and predominantly in never-smokers and patients of East Asian descent. This mutation increases kinase activity and sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Osimertinib, a third-generation EGFR TKI, is now the preferred first-line therapy based on the FLAURA trial, demonstrating superior PFS of 18.9 months and OS of 38.6 months. Earlier-generation TKIs including erlotinib and gefitinib remain alternatives but are associated with higher rates of acquired T790M resistance. NCCN guidelines recommend comprehensive biomarker testing including EGFR mutations before initiating therapy in advanced NSCLC. The EGFR L858R mutation is CIViC evidence level A for predicting response to EGFR TKI therapy.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to EGFR TKI therapy

text_summary: EGFR exon 19 deletions are the most common activating EGFR mutations, accounting for approximately 45% of EGFR-mutant NSCLC cases. These in-frame deletions remove the LREA motif in the kinase domain, resulting in constitutive receptor activation and strong sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Patients with exon 19 deletions generally have better outcomes with EGFR TKIs compared to L858R, with osimertinib showing median PFS of 21.4 months in the FLAURA trial for exon 19 del specifically. Osimertinib is the NCCN-preferred first-line agent. The most common resistance mechanism is EGFR T790M (occurring in ~60% of cases progressing on first/second-gen TKIs), which is itself targetable by osimertinib. EGFR exon 19 deletion is CIViC evidence level A with FDA-approved companion diagnostics.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to EGFR TKI therapy

text_summary: ALK rearrangements, most commonly the EML4-ALK fusion, occur in approximately 3-5% of NSCLC cases and define a distinct molecular subset of lung adenocarcinoma. ALK-positive NSCLC typically presents in younger, never-smoking patients. Alectinib is the NCCN-preferred first-line ALK inhibitor based on the ALEX trial, which demonstrated median PFS of 34.8 months versus 10.9 months for crizotinib. The CROWN trial established lorlatinib as an alternative first-line option with a 3-year PFS rate of 64%. Crizotinib was the first FDA-approved ALK inhibitor but is now generally reserved for later lines. Sequential use of ALK TKIs (crizotinib to alectinib/ceritinib to lorlatinib) can provide extended disease control. The most common resistance mutation is ALK G1202R, which confers resistance to most ALK TKIs except lorlatinib.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to ALK inhibitor therapy

text_summary: ROS1 rearrangements occur in approximately 1-2% of NSCLC and define a molecularly distinct subtype with sensitivity to ROS1-directed tyrosine kinase inhibitors. The most common fusion partner is CD74-ROS1. Crizotinib was the first FDA-approved therapy for ROS1-positive NSCLC with an ORR of 72% and median PFS of 19.3 months. Entrectinib received FDA approval based on integrated analysis showing ORR of 67% with intracranial activity. Repotrectinib has emerged as a next-generation option with activity against the G2032R solvent-front resistance mutation. NCCN guidelines recommend ROS1 testing as part of broad molecular profiling for all advanced NSCLC patients. FISH and NGS are acceptable testing methods for ROS1 rearrangement detection. ROS1-positive NSCLC is CIViC evidence level A with established clinical significance.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to ROS1 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: KRAS G12C is a gain-of-function mutation found in approximately 13% of NSCLC and 3% of colorectal cancers, historically considered undruggable until the development of covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors. Sotorasib (AMG 510) received FDA accelerated approval for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC based on the CodeBreaK 100 trial, demonstrating an ORR of 37.1% and median PFS of 6.8 months. Adagrasib (MRTX849) showed similar efficacy with ORR of 42.9% in the KRYSTAL-1 trial. Both agents lock KRAS G12C in its inactive GDP-bound state. Resistance mechanisms include secondary KRAS mutations (Y96D, R68S, H95D/Q/R), MET amplification, and RAS-MAPK pathway reactivation. In CRC, KRAS G12C inhibitors show lower single-agent activity, and combination strategies with anti-EGFR antibodies are under investigation. KRAS G12C is evidence level A in NSCLC with FDA-approved indications.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to KRAS G12C inhibitors

text_summary: HER2 (ERBB2) amplification is found in approximately 15-20% of breast cancers and defines HER2-positive breast cancer, a biologically aggressive subtype with distinct therapeutic implications. Trastuzumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting the HER2 extracellular domain, revolutionized treatment and is the backbone of HER2-directed therapy. Pertuzumab combined with trastuzumab and chemotherapy is the standard first-line regimen for metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer based on the CLEOPATRA trial, with median OS exceeding 56 months. Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) has shown remarkable activity in HER2-positive and HER2-low breast cancer (DESTINY-Breast03 and DESTINY-Breast04 trials). HER2 amplification is also actionable in gastric, colorectal, and NSCLC. Testing by IHC and FISH is mandated by NCCN guidelines for all breast cancers. HER2 amplification is CIViC evidence level A.
drugs: trastuzumab, pertuzumab, trastuzumab deruxtecan, trastuzumab emtansine, tucatinib
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to HER2-directed therapy

text_summary: NTRK gene fusions (NTRK1, NTRK2, NTRK3) are rare oncogenic drivers found across multiple tumor types at low frequency (typically less than 1%), but are highly enriched in certain rare cancers including secretory breast carcinoma, infantile fibrosarcoma, and congenital mesoblastic nephroma. Larotrectinib received the first tumor-agnostic FDA approval for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors based on the NAVIGATE, SCOUT, and LOXO-TRK-14001 trials, demonstrating an ORR of 75% across 17 cancer types. Entrectinib also received pan-cancer approval with ORR of 57% and CNS activity. These represent landmark tissue-agnostic approvals based entirely on molecular biomarker status regardless of histology. Resistance mechanisms include NTRK solvent-front mutations (G595R, G667C) and xDFG mutations. Next-generation TRK inhibitors including selitrectinib and repotrectinib are in development to address acquired resistance.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to TRK inhibitor therapy (tissue-agnostic)

text_summary: RET rearrangements occur in approximately 1-2% of NSCLC, 10-20% of papillary thyroid carcinomas, and are found sporadically in other solid tumors. The most common fusion partners in NSCLC are KIF5B-RET and CCDC6-RET. Selpercatinib, a highly selective RET inhibitor, received FDA approval based on the LIBRETTO-001 trial demonstrating ORR of 64% in previously treated and 84% in treatment-naive NSCLC patients. Pralsetinib showed similar efficacy in the ARROW trial with ORR of 61% in previously treated patients. Both agents demonstrate intracranial activity. RET fusions are mutually exclusive with other oncogenic drivers (EGFR, ALK, ROS1) in NSCLC. Resistance mechanisms include RET solvent-front mutations (G810R/S/C) and MET amplification. NCCN guidelines include selpercatinib and pralsetinib as first-line options for RET fusion-positive NSCLC.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to RET inhibitor therapy

text_summary: MET exon 14 skipping mutations occur in approximately 3-4% of NSCLC and result in decreased MET protein degradation, leading to constitutive MET signaling. These mutations are more common in older patients and in sarcomatoid histology. Capmatinib received FDA approval based on the GEOMETRY mono-1 trial, showing ORR of 68% in treatment-naive and 41% in previously treated patients with MET exon 14 skipping. Tepotinib showed comparable results in the VISION trial with ORR of 46%. Both are oral selective MET inhibitors. MET exon 14 skipping is distinct from MET amplification, which can occur as a primary driver or as a resistance mechanism to EGFR TKIs. Resistance to MET inhibitors can develop through secondary MET kinase domain mutations (D1228V/N, Y1230H/C) or bypass signaling through KRAS or EGFR activation.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to MET inhibitor therapy

text_summary: FGFR2 fusions occur in approximately 10-16% of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) and represent the most actionable molecular alteration in biliary tract cancers. Common fusion partners include BICC1, PPHLN1, and AHCYL1. Pemigatinib received FDA approval for FGFR2 fusion-positive cholangiocarcinoma based on the FIGHT-202 trial, demonstrating an ORR of 36% and median PFS of 6.9 months in previously treated patients. Futibatinib is another approved FGFR inhibitor for this indication based on the TAS-120-101 trial. Infigratinib also received conditional approval. FGFR2 fusions are typically identified by NGS and confirmed by FISH or RNA-based assays. Resistance mechanisms include FGFR2 kinase domain gatekeeper mutations (V564F) and molecular brake mutations. NCCN guidelines recommend FGFR2 testing in all cholangiocarcinoma patients.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to FGFR inhibitor therapy

text_summary: PIK3CA mutations are found in approximately 40% of HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancers, with hotspot mutations in the helical domain (E545K, E542K) and kinase domain (H1047R) accounting for the majority. Alpelisib, an alpha-specific PI3K inhibitor, received FDA approval in combination with fulvestrant for PIK3CA-mutant, HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer based on the SOLAR-1 trial, which showed PFS improvement from 5.7 to 11.0 months. The companion diagnostic therascreen PIK3CA RGQ PCR Kit is required for patient selection. Common adverse effects include hyperglycemia (requiring baseline HbA1c screening), rash, and diarrhea. PIK3CA mutations also occur in other cancers including endometrial, cervical, and head and neck squamous cell carcinoma. PIK3CA activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and frequently co-occurs with other oncogenic alterations.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to PI3K inhibitor therapy

text_summary: IDH1 R132H is a neomorphic gain-of-function mutation found in approximately 6-10% of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and 70-80% of WHO grade 2-3 gliomas. This mutation results in production of the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG), which causes widespread epigenetic dysregulation through inhibition of alpha-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Ivosidenib, a first-in-class oral IDH1 inhibitor, received FDA approval for IDH1-mutant relapsed/refractory AML based on the AG120-C-001 trial, with a complete remission rate of 24.7%. In newly diagnosed AML patients unfit for intensive chemotherapy, ivosidenib plus azacitidine significantly improved outcomes. Ivosidenib also received approval for IDH1-mutant cholangiocarcinoma based on the ClarIDHy trial. IDH1 testing by NGS or specific mutation assays is recommended at AML diagnosis per NCCN guidelines.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to IDH1 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: IDH2 R140Q is the most common IDH2 mutation in acute myeloid leukemia, occurring in approximately 8-12% of AML cases. Like IDH1 mutations, IDH2 R140Q produces the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate, leading to a hypermethylation phenotype and impaired hematopoietic differentiation. Enasidenib, an oral IDH2 inhibitor, received FDA approval for IDH2-mutant relapsed/refractory AML based on the AG221-C-001 trial, demonstrating an overall response rate of 40.3% and median OS of 9.3 months. Differentiation syndrome is a known toxicity requiring monitoring. IDH2 R140Q generally carries a more favorable prognosis in AML compared to wild-type IDH2. Both IDH1 and IDH2 mutations are mutually exclusive in AML. NCCN guidelines recommend IDH2 testing at diagnosis to guide treatment decisions in AML.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to IDH2 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: BRCA1 pathogenic mutations are found in approximately 5-10% of breast cancers, 15-20% of ovarian cancers, and at lower frequencies in prostate, pancreatic, and other cancers. These loss-of-function mutations impair homologous recombination DNA repair, creating synthetic lethality with PARP inhibition. Olaparib received FDA approval for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer (SOLO-1 trial: PFS 56 months vs 13.8 months with placebo) and HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer with germline BRCA mutation (OlympiAD trial). Rucaparib is approved for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer based on the ARIEL3 trial. BRCA1 mutations are identified by germline and/or somatic NGS testing. Reversion mutations in BRCA1 that restore homologous recombination function are a key resistance mechanism to PARP inhibitors. BRCA1 status also predicts response to platinum-based chemotherapy.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to PARP inhibitor therapy

text_summary: BRCA2 pathogenic mutations confer deficiency in homologous recombination DNA repair and are found in approximately 5-7% of breast cancers, 6-12% of ovarian cancers, 5-7% of metastatic prostate cancers, and 5-10% of pancreatic cancers. Like BRCA1, BRCA2 loss creates synthetic lethality with PARP inhibition. Olaparib is FDA-approved for BRCA-mutant metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (PROfound trial), BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer, and germline BRCA-mutant breast cancer. Rucaparib has approval for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer. In pancreatic cancer, olaparib maintenance after platinum-based chemotherapy improved PFS in the POLO trial. BRCA2 mutations also predict sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy across tumor types. Both germline and somatic BRCA2 testing is recommended in eligible cancers per NCCN guidelines. Reversion mutations restoring BRCA2 open reading frame are a well-documented resistance mechanism.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to PARP inhibitor therapy

text_summary: ERBB2 (HER2) S310F is a recurrent activating missense mutation found in approximately 2-3% of bladder cancers, with additional occurrences in breast, lung, and other solid tumors. This mutation in the extracellular domain of HER2 results in constitutive receptor dimerization and downstream signaling activation independent of ligand binding. Clinical evidence from the MyPathway basket trial and other studies suggests sensitivity to HER2-directed therapies including neratinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan. In bladder cancer, HER2 mutations are being investigated as targets for ADC therapies and pan-HER inhibitors. ERBB2 S310F is categorized as CIViC evidence level B, with growing clinical trial evidence supporting its actionability. NGS-based tumor profiling is required for detection of point mutations versus amplification.
clinical_significance: Predicted sensitizing / emerging evidence for HER2-directed therapy

text_summary: KIT D816V is an activating mutation in the kinase domain activation loop found in approximately 80% of systemic mastocytosis and 5-10% of gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST). In GIST, KIT D816V confers primary resistance to imatinib. Avapritinib is a type I kinase inhibitor specifically designed to target the KIT D816V activation loop mutation and received FDA approval for GIST harboring PDGFRA D842V mutations. For advanced systemic mastocytosis, avapritinib received approval based on the EXPLORER and PATHFINDER trials demonstrating an ORR of 93% in advanced SM. Midostaurin is also active against KIT D816V. This mutation alters the ATP-binding pocket conformation, making it insensitive to type II inhibitors like imatinib but sensitive to type I inhibitors that bind the active kinase conformation.
clinical_significance: Resistance to imatinib / sensitizing to avapritinib

text_summary: PDGFRA D842V is the most common PDGFRA mutation in gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST), accounting for approximately 5-6% of all GIST and about 60-70% of PDGFRA-mutant GIST. This activation loop mutation confers primary resistance to imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib. Avapritinib received FDA approval specifically for PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST based on the NAVIGATOR trial, demonstrating an ORR of 88% and median duration of response not reached at data cutoff. Avapritinib is a type I kinase inhibitor that binds the active conformation of the kinase, unlike imatinib which requires the inactive conformation. NCCN guidelines recommend avapritinib as first-line therapy for PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST. Molecular testing for PDGFRA mutations should be performed in all imatinib-resistant or KIT wild-type GIST.
clinical_significance: Resistance to imatinib / sensitizing to avapritinib

text_summary: TP53 R175H is one of the most common TP53 missense mutations across all cancers, classified as a structural hotspot mutation that disrupts the DNA-binding domain and results in both loss of tumor-suppressor function and gain-of-function oncogenic properties. TP53 R175H is found at high frequency in ovarian, colorectal, breast, and pancreatic cancers. This mutation is generally associated with poor prognosis, resistance to DNA-damaging chemotherapy, and genomic instability. While no directly targeted therapy exists, TP53 R175H status informs prognosis and treatment selection. Emerging therapeutic strategies include the reactivation of mutant p53 (APR-246/eprenetapopt), synthetic lethality approaches (WEE1 inhibitors, CHK1 inhibitors), and immunotherapy, as TP53-mutant tumors often have higher TMB. TP53 R175H is classified as CIViC evidence level C/D with prognostic significance.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / adverse prognosis, no approved targeted therapy

text_summary: PTEN loss through deletion, mutation, or epigenetic silencing is one of the most frequently altered tumor suppressors in human cancers, occurring in approximately 30-40% of endometrial cancers, 15-20% of glioblastomas, 10-20% of prostate cancers, and at varying frequencies in breast, colorectal, and other malignancies. Loss of PTEN function activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway, promoting cell survival, proliferation, and metabolic reprogramming. PTEN loss is associated with resistance to multiple therapies including EGFR inhibitors and hormonal therapy. While no direct PTEN-targeting therapy exists, PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors (alpelisib, everolimus, capivasertib) may be effective in PTEN-deficient tumors. PTEN loss is being investigated as a biomarker for AKT inhibitor sensitivity in clinical trials. PTEN status assessment by IHC and/or NGS is increasingly recommended.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / potential sensitivity to PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitors

text_summary: CDKN2A homozygous deletion is a frequent genomic alteration found in approximately 30-40% of mesotheliomas, 15-20% of glioblastomas, 10-15% of pancreatic cancers, and in subsets of NSCLC, melanoma, and other solid tumors. CDKN2A encodes both p16INK4a and p14ARF, which regulate the RB and p53 tumor suppressor pathways respectively. Loss of p16INK4a removes an important CDK4/6 inhibitory checkpoint, leading to unchecked cell cycle progression. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are being investigated in CDKN2A-deleted tumors beyond breast cancer. In mesothelioma, CDKN2A deletion detected by FISH is a diagnostic marker distinguishing malignant mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferation. CDKN2A deletion is prognostic and associated with aggressive clinical behavior across multiple tumor types.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / investigational CDK4/6 inhibitor target

text_summary: STK11 (LKB1) inactivating mutations occur in approximately 15-20% of NSCLC and are associated with an immunologically cold tumor microenvironment, lower PD-L1 expression, and poor response to immune checkpoint inhibitors. STK11 mutations are enriched in KRAS-mutant NSCLC and define a subset (KRAS/STK11 co-mutant) with particularly poor immunotherapy outcomes. STK11 loss leads to dysregulation of AMPK signaling, resulting in metabolic reprogramming and mTOR activation. The KEAP1/STK11 co-mutation pattern in NSCLC is associated with the worst prognosis. Emerging strategies for STK11-mutant tumors include STING agonists, metabolic intervention with metformin analogs, and combination immunotherapy approaches. STK11 mutation status is increasingly recognized as a predictive biomarker for immunotherapy resistance in NSCLC and is included in comprehensive genomic profiling panels.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / resistance to immunotherapy

text_summary: ESR1 mutations are acquired activating mutations in the estrogen receptor alpha gene found in approximately 20-40% of metastatic HR-positive breast cancers that progress on aromatase inhibitor therapy. Hotspot mutations (D538G, Y537S, Y537N, L536R) in the ligand-binding domain result in constitutive receptor activation independent of estrogen ligand. ESR1 mutations confer resistance to aromatase inhibitors but retain partial sensitivity to selective estrogen receptor degraders (SERDs) like fulvestrant and elacestrant. Elacestrant received FDA approval for ESR1-mutant, ER-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer based on the EMERALD trial. ESR1 mutations can be detected in ctDNA (liquid biopsy), enabling non-invasive serial monitoring of resistance emergence. NCCN guidelines recommend ESR1 mutation testing by liquid biopsy at progression on endocrine therapy. Novel oral SERDs are in clinical development to address this resistance mechanism.
clinical_significance: Resistance to aromatase inhibitors / sensitizing to SERDs

text_summary: APC truncating mutations are the initiating genomic event in approximately 70-80% of colorectal cancers and are the hallmark of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP). APC is a key negative regulator of the WNT/beta-catenin signaling pathway; its loss leads to constitutive beta-catenin nuclear translocation and transcription of proliferative genes. While APC mutations are not directly targetable with approved therapies, they define the molecular pathogenesis of the CIN (chromosomal instability) pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis. APC mutation status is relevant for FAP screening and genetic counseling. Emerging therapeutic approaches include tankyrase inhibitors that target downstream WNT signaling and beta-catenin degraders. APC mutations frequently co-occur with TP53, KRAS, and SMAD4 mutations in the classical adenoma-carcinoma sequence of colorectal cancer.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / pathogenic driver, no approved targeted therapy

text_summary: POLE exonuclease domain mutations are rare alterations (approximately 1-2% of endometrial cancers, less than 1% of CRC) that result in a hypermutation phenotype with extremely high tumor mutational burden (typically greater than 100 mutations per megabase). These proofreading domain mutations (P286R, V411L are hotspots) lead to error-prone DNA replication and generate an abundance of neoantigens. POLE-mutant tumors are associated with favorable prognosis in endometrial cancer and may respond exceptionally well to immune checkpoint inhibitors due to their high neoantigen burden. In endometrial cancer, POLE mutation defines a distinct molecular subtype (POLE ultramutated) per The Cancer Genome Atlas classification. NCCN guidelines incorporate POLE status into risk stratification for endometrial cancer. Detection requires NGS with specific analysis of the exonuclease domain hotspot regions.
clinical_significance: Favorable prognosis / immunotherapy response

text_summary: Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) status results from deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) caused by germline or somatic inactivation of MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 genes, or by MLH1 promoter hypermethylation. MSI-H occurs in approximately 15% of colorectal cancers, 20-30% of endometrial cancers, and at lower frequencies in gastric, ovarian, and other cancers. Pembrolizumab received the first tissue-agnostic FDA approval for MSI-H/dMMR unresectable or metastatic solid tumors based on five single-arm clinical trials demonstrating ORR of 39.6%. The KEYNOTE-177 trial established pembrolizumab as first-line therapy for MSI-H metastatic CRC with PFS of 16.5 months versus 8.2 months for chemotherapy. MSI testing is performed by PCR (Bethesda markers) or NGS, and dMMR by IHC for MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2. Universal MSI/MMR testing is recommended for all CRC patients per NCCN guidelines.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (tissue-agnostic)

text_summary: Tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H), defined as 10 or more mutations per megabase, is a pan-cancer predictive biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor response. Pembrolizumab received FDA approval for TMB-H unresectable or metastatic solid tumors based on the KEYNOTE-158 trial, which demonstrated ORR of 29% in TMB-high versus 6% in TMB-low patients across 10 tumor types. TMB-H is measured by comprehensive genomic profiling using validated NGS assays such as FoundationOne CDx. TMB varies significantly by cancer type, with melanoma, NSCLC, and bladder cancer typically having higher median TMB. The relationship between TMB and immunotherapy response is strongest in cancers where the mutational landscape generates immunogenic neoantigens. TMB-H can be driven by smoking-related mutational signatures, mismatch repair deficiency, POLE mutations, or other mechanisms of genomic instability. TMB should be interpreted in the context of other biomarkers including PD-L1 and MSI status.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (tissue-agnostic)

text_summary: PALB2 (Partner and Localizer of BRCA2) pathogenic mutations are found in approximately 1-2% of breast cancers, 3-4% of pancreatic cancers, and at lower frequencies in ovarian and prostate cancers. PALB2 is a key component of the homologous recombination DNA repair pathway, bridging BRCA1 and BRCA2 in the repair complex. Loss-of-function PALB2 mutations confer homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) and synthetic lethality with PARP inhibition, similar to BRCA1/2 mutations. Olaparib has demonstrated activity in PALB2-mutant cancers, and PALB2 is included in expanded HRD-related gene panels for PARP inhibitor eligibility. Germline PALB2 mutations confer a moderate-to-high risk of breast cancer (approximately 3-5 fold increased risk) and are included in multigene hereditary cancer panels. NCCN guidelines classify PALB2 as a high-penetrance breast cancer susceptibility gene warranting enhanced screening.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to PARP inhibitors, HRD biomarker

text_summary: ATM (Ataxia Telangiectasia Mutated) inactivating mutations are found in approximately 5-10% of metastatic prostate cancers, 5-8% of pancreatic cancers, and at lower frequencies in breast, ovarian, and gastric cancers. ATM is a central kinase in the DNA damage response, coordinating cell-cycle arrest and DNA repair following double-strand breaks. Biallelic ATM loss impairs homologous recombination and may create sensitivity to PARP inhibitors, although clinical evidence is less robust than for BRCA1/2 mutations. The PROfound trial included ATM-mutant prostate cancer patients in the olaparib arm, but the benefit was driven primarily by the BRCA-mutant subgroup. ATM mutations may sensitize tumors to platinum-based chemotherapy and ATR inhibitors (ceralasertib). ATM status is included in comprehensive genomic profiling panels and DDR (DNA damage response) gene panels. Further investigation is needed to define the optimal therapeutic strategy for ATM-mutant cancers.
clinical_significance: Potential sensitivity to PARP inhibitors and DDR-targeted therapy

text_summary: ARID1A inactivating mutations are found in approximately 40-50% of ovarian clear cell carcinoma, 30-40% of endometrial cancers, 10-15% of gastric cancers, and in subsets of cholangiocarcinoma and bladder cancer. ARID1A is a key subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, and its loss disrupts epigenetic regulation of gene expression. ARID1A-mutant tumors show synthetic lethality with EZH2 inhibition (tazemetostat), ATR inhibition, and may have enhanced sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors due to increased tumor immunogenicity through upregulation of interferon signaling. The ARID1A-EZH2 synthetic lethality axis is under active clinical investigation. ARID1A loss by IHC correlates with mutation status and is a rapid screening tool. Emerging evidence suggests ARID1A-mutant tumors may benefit from immunotherapy, particularly in endometrial and gastric cancers where ARID1A loss is associated with higher TMB and MSI.
clinical_significance: Investigational / potential immunotherapy and EZH2 inhibitor target

text_summary: The CD74-NRG1 fusion is a rare oncogenic driver found in approximately 0.2-0.5% of invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas of the lung and at low frequency in pancreatic, breast, and gallbladder cancers. This fusion juxtaposes the CD74 transmembrane domain with the NRG1 EGF-like domain, resulting in autocrine/paracrine activation of HER3 (ERBB3) and downstream PI3K/AKT and MAPK signaling. Because NRG1 fusions signal through HER2-HER3 heterodimers, therapeutic strategies focus on HER2/HER3 blockade. Afatinib (pan-HER inhibitor) has shown clinical activity in case reports and small series of NRG1 fusion-positive lung cancers. Zenocutuzumab, a bispecific HER2xHER3 antibody that blocks NRG1-driven HER2-HER3 dimerization, received FDA accelerated approval for NRG1 fusion-positive NSCLC, pancreatic, and other solid tumors based on the eNRGy trial with ORR of approximately 33%. NRG1 fusions are detected by RNA-based NGS and are typically mutually exclusive with other oncogenic drivers in NSCLC. Testing is recommended particularly in invasive mucinous lung adenocarcinoma with no other identified driver.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to HER2-HER3 directed therapy

text_summary: TROP2 (TACSTD2) overexpression is a cell-surface glycoprotein biomarker broadly expressed across epithelial cancers, with high expression found in approximately 80-90% of triple-negative breast cancers, 60-80% of HR-positive breast cancers, and 60-70% of urothelial carcinomas. TROP2 promotes tumor proliferation, invasion, and metastasis through intracellular signaling involving cyclin D1 and ERK activation. TROP2 overexpression serves as the therapeutic target for the antibody-drug conjugate sacituzumab govitecan (anti-TROP2 ADC with SN-38 payload), which is FDA-approved for metastatic TNBC (ASCENT trial: PFS 5.6 vs 1.7 months), HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer (TROPiCS-02 trial), and urothelial carcinoma. Unlike HER2 testing, TROP2 expression is not used as a patient selection biomarker for sacituzumab govitecan, as the drug demonstrates activity across a range of TROP2 expression levels. Datopotamab deruxtecan is another TROP2-directed ADC under investigation. TROP2 expression is assessed by IHC using the SP419 clone.
clinical_significance: Therapeutic target for anti-TROP2 ADCs

text_summary: Nectin-4 (PVRL4) overexpression is found in approximately 60-70% of urothelial carcinomas and at varying frequencies in breast, lung, pancreatic, and gastric cancers. Nectin-4 is a type I transmembrane protein belonging to the immunoglobulin superfamily that mediates cell adhesion and serves as a viral entry receptor. Its overexpression in urothelial carcinoma makes it an ideal target for the antibody-drug conjugate enfortumab vedotin, which links an anti-Nectin-4 antibody to the microtubule-disrupting agent MMAE via a protease-cleavable linker. Enfortumab vedotin received FDA approval for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, and the EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 trial established enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab as the new first-line standard with median PFS of 12.5 months. Nectin-4 expression by IHC is not required for patient selection, as enfortumab vedotin is approved regardless of expression level. Nectin-4 loss through downregulation or mutation is an emerging resistance mechanism.
clinical_significance: Therapeutic target for anti-Nectin-4 ADC therapy

text_summary: Folate receptor alpha (FRalpha, FOLR1) overexpression is present in approximately 80% of high-grade serous ovarian cancers and at lower frequencies in endometrial, TNBC, and NSCLC. FRalpha is a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored membrane protein that mediates cellular uptake of folate and is largely restricted to the apical surface of normal epithelial cells, making it an attractive therapeutic target with limited normal-tissue toxicity. Mirvetuximab soravtansine, an anti-FRalpha ADC with the tubulin-disrupting DM4 payload, received FDA accelerated approval for FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian cancer based on the SORAYA trial (ORR 32%) and was further validated in the MIRASOL trial (PFS 5.6 vs 4.0 months vs chemotherapy). FRalpha expression is assessed by IHC using the VENTANA FOLR1 (26B3) RxDx assay, with positivity defined as membrane staining at 2+ or 3+ in at least 75% of viable tumor cells (PS2+ score). Patient selection by FRalpha IHC is mandatory for mirvetuximab eligibility.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to anti-FRalpha ADC therapy

text_summary: Claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2) is a tight junction protein normally expressed on differentiated gastric mucosal cells that becomes aberrantly exposed on the cell surface in approximately 38-40% of gastric/gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) adenocarcinomas and 50-60% of pancreatic cancers. During malignant transformation, loss of cell polarity exposes CLDN18.2 epitopes that are normally hidden in tight junctions, making it accessible to antibody-based therapies. Zolbetuximab is a chimeric anti-CLDN18.2 monoclonal antibody that induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC). The SPOTLIGHT and GLOW trials demonstrated that zolbetuximab combined with chemotherapy significantly improved PFS and OS in CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative gastric/GEJ cancer. CLDN18.2 expression is assessed by IHC using the VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RxDx assay, with positivity defined as moderate-to-strong membrane staining in at least 75% of tumor cells.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to anti-CLDN18.2 therapy

text_summary: DLL3 (Delta-like ligand 3) is an inhibitory Notch ligand aberrantly expressed on the cell surface of approximately 80-85% of small cell lung cancers (SCLC) and other high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas, while having minimal expression on normal tissues. DLL3 is normally localized intracellularly where it inhibits Notch signaling, but in SCLC it is redirected to the cell surface through mechanisms related to neuroendocrine differentiation. DLL3 surface expression makes it an ideal target for DLL3-directed therapies. Tarlatamab, a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) that bridges DLL3 on tumor cells with CD3 on T cells, received FDA accelerated approval for previously treated extensive-stage SCLC based on the DeLLphi-301 trial showing ORR of 40% and median DOR of 9.7 months at the 10mg dose. Cytokine release syndrome and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity are key adverse effects. DLL3 expression is assessed by IHC but is not required for tarlatamab eligibility given the high prevalence in SCLC.
clinical_significance: Therapeutic target for DLL3-directed bispecific T-cell engagers

text_summary: KRAS G12D is the most common KRAS mutation across all cancers, found in approximately 36% of KRAS-mutant pancreatic cancers, 13% of KRAS-mutant colorectal cancers, and 4% of KRAS-mutant NSCLCs. Unlike KRAS G12C, which has a reactive cysteine enabling covalent inhibitor design, KRAS G12D lacks such a nucleophilic residue, making it historically more challenging to drug. However, recent advances have produced KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors including MRTX1133 and other non-covalent inhibitors that trap KRAS G12D in the inactive state. Early clinical trial data from the KRAS G12D inhibitor programs are showing promising activity in pancreatic and colorectal cancers. Adoptive cell therapy approaches using mutation-specific T-cell receptors targeting KRAS G12D have also shown clinical responses in case reports. KRAS G12D co-occurs frequently with TP53 and CDKN2A alterations in pancreatic cancer. The development of KRAS G12D inhibitors represents a major advance for pancreatic cancer, where this mutation is the dominant driver.
clinical_significance: Investigational / emerging KRAS G12D-targeted therapies

text_summary: MAP2K1 K57N is a recurrent activating mutation in the MEK1 kinase encoded by the MAP2K1 gene, found in approximately 5-8% of melanomas, 1-3% of NSCLCs, and in subsets of colorectal, ovarian, and hairy cell leukemias. This gain-of-function mutation in the negative regulatory helix of MEK1 results in constitutive kinase activity independent of upstream RAF activation. MAP2K1 K57N drives MAPK pathway signaling and is mutually exclusive with BRAF V600E in melanoma. MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib, binimetinib, selumetinib) have demonstrated clinical activity against MAP2K1-mutant tumors, though evidence is primarily from case series and small cohort studies. In the NCI-MATCH trial, selumetinib showed activity in MAP2K1-mutant cancers. MAP2K1 mutations may also mediate acquired resistance to BRAF inhibitors in BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma. Comprehensive genomic profiling by NGS is required for detection, and MAP2K1 mutations should be considered in the differential of BRAF/RAS wild-type tumors with MAPK activation.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to MEK inhibitors

text_summary: EZH2 Y646N (also annotated as Y641N) is a recurrent gain-of-function mutation in the SET domain of the EZH2 histone methyltransferase, found in approximately 7-12% of follicular lymphomas and 22% of germinal center B-cell (GCB) diffuse large B-cell lymphomas. This neomorphic mutation alters substrate specificity, increasing di-to-trimethylation of histone H3 at lysine 27 (H3K27me3), leading to transcriptional silencing of tumor suppressor genes including CDKN2A and genes involved in B-cell differentiation. Tazemetostat, a selective EZH2 inhibitor, received FDA approval for relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma with EZH2 mutation based on a phase 2 study demonstrating ORR of 69% (CR 12%) in EZH2-mutant FL. Tazemetostat is also approved for EZH2 wild-type FL patients who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options. The drug is well tolerated with manageable adverse events. EZH2 mutation testing by NGS is recommended for FL patients to guide treatment selection.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to EZH2 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: SMARCB1 (INI1/SNF5/BAF47) biallelic loss through homozygous deletion or truncating mutations is the defining molecular feature of several aggressive tumor types including malignant rhabdoid tumors (MRT), epithelioid sarcoma, renal medullary carcinoma, and a subset of poorly differentiated chordomas. SMARCB1 is a core subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, and its loss results in epigenetic dysregulation with aberrant gene silencing. Loss of SMARCB1 creates a dependency on EZH2-mediated transcriptional repression, establishing a synthetic lethal relationship. Tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) received FDA approval for metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma not eligible for curative surgery, based on a phase 2 trial demonstrating ORR of 15% with durable responses. SMARCB1 loss is detected by IHC (loss of INI1 nuclear staining) or by NGS for biallelic inactivation. SMARCB1 status assessment is diagnostic and therapeutic, as it both confirms the diagnosis and identifies potential EZH2 inhibitor eligibility.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / sensitizing to EZH2 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: CDH1 germline truncating mutations (nonsense, frameshift, splice-site) are the causative genetic alterations in hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC) syndrome. Pathogenic CDH1 variants confer a lifetime risk of approximately 56-70% for diffuse gastric cancer and 39-55% for lobular breast cancer in female carriers. CDH1 encodes E-cadherin, a calcium-dependent cell adhesion molecule critical for epithelial tissue architecture and cell polarity. Loss of E-cadherin disrupts cell-cell adhesion, promoting the infiltrative growth pattern characteristic of diffuse/signet-ring cell gastric cancer and invasive lobular breast carcinoma. NCCN guidelines recommend prophylactic total gastrectomy for confirmed pathogenic CDH1 mutation carriers, typically between ages 20 and 30 years. Annual breast MRI and mammography screening is recommended for female carriers. Genetic counseling is essential for at-risk family members. Somatic CDH1 loss also occurs in sporadic lobular breast cancer and diffuse gastric cancer through mutation, LOH, or promoter methylation.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / hereditary diffuse gastric cancer syndrome, prophylactic gastrectomy recommended

text_summary: RAD51C truncating (pathogenic) mutations are found in approximately 1-2% of ovarian cancers and less than 1% of breast cancers. RAD51C is a critical component of the homologous recombination DNA repair pathway, functioning in RAD51 filament formation and Holliday junction resolution. Biallelic RAD51C loss impairs homologous recombination repair, contributing to genomic instability and establishing a homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) phenotype analogous to BRCA1/2 loss. RAD51C-deficient tumors are expected to exhibit synthetic lethality with PARP inhibition, and RAD51C is included in the HRR gene panels used for PARP inhibitor eligibility in ovarian and prostate cancers. The ARIEL3 and PRIMA trials included RAD51C-mutant patients in their HRD-positive cohorts showing PARP inhibitor benefit. Germline RAD51C mutations confer a moderate-to-high risk of ovarian cancer (approximately 5-10% lifetime risk) and are included in hereditary cancer multigene panels. NCCN guidelines recommend risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy for RAD51C carriers.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to PARP inhibitors, HRD biomarker

text_summary: BARD1 truncating mutations are rare pathogenic variants found in approximately 0.5-1% of breast cancers and at lower frequencies in ovarian cancer. BARD1 (BRCA1-Associated RING Domain 1) forms a constitutive heterodimer with BRCA1 through their respective RING domains, and this BRCA1-BARD1 complex is essential for homologous recombination DNA repair, ubiquitin ligase activity, and tumor suppression. Loss of BARD1 destabilizes BRCA1 and impairs the homologous recombination pathway, creating an HRD phenotype. BARD1 is classified as a moderate-penetrance breast cancer susceptibility gene by NCCN, with an estimated two- to four-fold increased lifetime breast cancer risk. BARD1-deficient tumors may respond to PARP inhibitors through synthetic lethality, similar to BRCA-deficient cancers, though prospective clinical trial data are limited. BARD1 is included in expanded HRR gene panels and multigene hereditary cancer panels. Functional studies confirm that pathogenic BARD1 variants impair BRCA1-dependent DNA repair foci formation.
clinical_significance: Potential sensitivity to PARP inhibitors, moderate-penetrance cancer susceptibility

text_summary: CTLA-4 (Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-Associated Protein 4) is a co-inhibitory immune checkpoint receptor expressed on T cells that competes with CD28 for binding to B7 ligands (CD80/CD86) on antigen-presenting cells, dampening T-cell activation. Unlike variant-based biomarkers, CTLA-4 is a drug target rather than a somatic tumor alteration. Ipilimumab, a fully human anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibody, blocks CTLA-4-mediated immune suppression and enhances T-cell priming and activation in lymph nodes. Ipilimumab was the first immune checkpoint inhibitor to demonstrate overall survival benefit in metastatic melanoma (MDX010-20 trial, 2010). The combination of ipilimumab with nivolumab (anti-PD-1) achieves synergistic immune activation through complementary mechanisms: ipilimumab enhances T-cell priming while nivolumab prevents T-cell exhaustion in the tumor microenvironment. This combination is FDA-approved in melanoma (CheckMate-067: 5-year OS 52%), renal cell carcinoma (CheckMate-214), NSCLC, hepatocellular carcinoma, and mesothelioma. CTLA-4 blockade is associated with higher rates of immune-related adverse events compared to PD-1 monotherapy.
clinical_significance: Immune checkpoint target / ipilimumab enhances anti-tumor immunity

text_summary: FLT3 internal tandem duplications (ITD) occur in approximately 25-30% of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and represent one of the most clinically significant molecular alterations in this disease. FLT3-ITD results in constitutive activation of the FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase, driving leukemic cell proliferation and survival through STAT5, RAS/MAPK, and PI3K/AKT pathways. The allelic ratio of FLT3-ITD to wild-type FLT3 is prognostically significant, with high allelic ratios (greater than 0.5) conferring adverse prognosis per ELN 2022 guidelines. Midostaurin combined with standard induction chemotherapy received FDA approval based on the RATIFY trial, which demonstrated improved OS (HR 0.78) in FLT3-mutant AML. Gilteritinib, a more selective FLT3 inhibitor, is approved for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML based on the ADMIRAL trial (OS 9.3 vs 5.6 months). Quizartinib received FDA approval for newly diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML in combination with chemotherapy based on the QuANTUM-First trial.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to FLT3 inhibitor therapy, adverse prognosis

text_summary: FLT3 D835 tyrosine kinase domain (TKD) point mutations occur in approximately 5-10% of AML cases and result in constitutive kinase activation through disruption of the activation loop autoinhibitory conformation. Unlike FLT3-ITD, TKD mutations such as D835Y, D835V, and D835H confer a less clearly adverse prognosis, and ELN 2022 classifies FLT3-TKD mutations as intermediate risk. FLT3 TKD mutations are sensitive to midostaurin and gilteritinib but are resistant to type II FLT3 inhibitors such as quizartinib and sorafenib, which require the inactive DFG-out kinase conformation for binding. Gilteritinib, a type I inhibitor, retains activity against D835 mutations and is the preferred agent for relapsed/refractory FLT3-TKD-mutant AML. FLT3 D835 mutations can also emerge as acquired resistance mutations during treatment with type II FLT3 inhibitors.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to type I FLT3 inhibitors, resistance to type II inhibitors

text_summary: NPM1 mutations are the most common genetic alteration in AML, occurring in approximately 30-35% of cases, with the type A insertion (TCTG duplication in exon 12) accounting for 75-80% of NPM1 mutations. NPM1 mutations cause aberrant cytoplasmic localization of the nucleophosmin protein (NPMc+), disrupting its normal nucleolar functions in ribosome biogenesis and genomic stability. NPM1-mutant AML without FLT3-ITD is classified as favorable risk by ELN 2022, with 5-year OS rates of 60-70% with intensive chemotherapy. NPM1 mutation is a stable marker for measurable residual disease (MRD) monitoring by RT-qPCR, and MRD-guided treatment decisions are increasingly incorporated into clinical practice. NPM1-mutant AML shows sensitivity to venetoclax-based regimens, and the combination of venetoclax plus azacitidine has demonstrated high response rates in NPM1-mutant elderly AML patients unfit for intensive chemotherapy.
clinical_significance: Favorable prognosis / MRD monitoring marker

text_summary: CEBPA biallelic mutations (biCEBPA) occur in approximately 4-6% of AML and define a distinct WHO entity (AML with biallelic CEBPA mutations) associated with favorable prognosis. Biallelic mutations typically consist of an N-terminal frameshift mutation that eliminates the p42 isoform combined with a C-terminal in-frame insertion/deletion that disrupts the bZIP dimerization domain. ELN 2022 classifies biCEBPA-mutant AML as favorable risk with 5-year OS rates of 55-65% with intensive chemotherapy alone. Single (monoallelic) CEBPA mutations do not confer the same favorable prognosis and are classified as intermediate risk. CEBPA mutations are associated with a distinct gene expression profile and are mutually exclusive with NPM1 mutations. Germline CEBPA mutations predispose to familial AML with high penetrance, and genetic counseling should be considered in young patients with CEBPA mutations.
clinical_significance: Favorable prognosis / defines distinct AML entity

text_summary: The BCR-ABL1 fusion results from the t(9;22)(q34;q11.2) Philadelphia chromosome translocation and is the defining molecular alteration in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), present in over 95% of cases. The resulting BCR-ABL1 oncoprotein is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives myeloid proliferation through STAT5, RAS/MAPK, and PI3K/AKT pathways. Imatinib, the first-in-class BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor, transformed CML from a fatal disease to a chronic condition with 10-year OS exceeding 85%. Second-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib) show deeper molecular responses and are NCCN-preferred first-line options. Ponatinib is effective against the T315I gatekeeper resistance mutation. Asciminib, an allosteric BCR-ABL1 inhibitor targeting the myristoyl pocket (STAMP inhibitor), received FDA approval for CML with T315I or after failure of two or more prior TKIs based on the ASCEMBL trial.
drugs: imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib, ponatinib, asciminib
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to BCR-ABL1 TKI therapy

text_summary: JAK2 V617F is a somatic gain-of-function mutation found in approximately 95% of polycythemia vera (PV), 55-65% of essential thrombocythemia (ET), and 55-65% of primary myelofibrosis (PMF). This mutation in the pseudokinase domain of JAK2 disrupts autoinhibition, resulting in constitutive JAK-STAT signaling that drives myeloproliferation. JAK2 V617F is a major diagnostic criterion for BCR-ABL1-negative myeloproliferative neoplasms per WHO 2022 classification. Ruxolitinib, a JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, is FDA-approved for intermediate- and high-risk myelofibrosis (COMFORT-I/II trials) and hydroxyurea-resistant/intolerant PV (RESPONSE trial). Fedratinib is approved for intermediate-2 or high-risk myelofibrosis. The variant allele frequency (VAF) of JAK2 V617F correlates with disease phenotype: lower VAF is associated with ET while higher VAF favors PV or MF. Molecular monitoring of JAK2 V617F VAF is used to assess treatment response.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / sensitizing to JAK inhibitor therapy

text_summary: CALR (calreticulin) exon 9 frameshift mutations occur in approximately 25-30% of essential thrombocythemia and 25-30% of primary myelofibrosis, and are mutually exclusive with JAK2 and MPL mutations. The two most common variants are type 1 (52-bp deletion, L367fs*46) and type 2 (5-bp insertion, K385fs*47), which together account for over 80% of CALR mutations. Mutant CALR acquires a novel C-terminal domain that enables pathogenic binding to the thrombopoietin receptor (MPL), causing constitutive JAK-STAT pathway activation independent of ligand binding. CALR mutations are a major diagnostic criterion for BCR-ABL1-negative MPN per WHO 2022. Type 1 CALR mutations in myelofibrosis are associated with more favorable prognosis compared to type 2 or JAK2 V617F. JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib, fedratinib) are active in CALR-mutant MPN, as the downstream signaling converges on JAK-STAT activation regardless of the initiating mutation.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / favorable prognosis (type 1), JAK inhibitor responsive

text_summary: MPL W515L is a gain-of-function mutation in the thrombopoietin receptor found in approximately 3-5% of essential thrombocythemia and 5-10% of primary myelofibrosis. This mutation in the transmembrane-juxtamembrane junction of MPL results in constitutive receptor activation and downstream JAK-STAT signaling independent of thrombopoietin ligand. MPL W515L is a WHO 2022 diagnostic criterion for BCR-ABL1-negative MPN and is mutually exclusive with JAK2 V617F and CALR mutations. MPL-mutant MPN responds to JAK inhibitor therapy, and ruxolitinib is FDA-approved for myelofibrosis regardless of MPL mutation status based on the COMFORT trials. MPL W515K is another activating mutation at the same codon. Patients with MPL-mutant ET tend to have higher platelet counts and lower hemoglobin compared to JAK2-mutant ET, reflecting distinct disease biology.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / MPN classification, JAK inhibitor responsive

text_summary: DNMT3A R882H is the most common DNMT3A mutation in AML, occurring in approximately 15-25% of cases. DNMT3A R882H acts as a dominant-negative mutation that impairs DNA methyltransferase activity, leading to focal hypomethylation at CpG sites and dysregulated gene expression in hematopoietic stem cells. DNMT3A mutations are frequently founding/early clonal events in leukemogenesis and are commonly detected in clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). ELN 2022 does not assign a specific risk category to DNMT3A mutations alone, but they are associated with inferior outcomes when co-occurring with NPM1 and FLT3-ITD mutations. DNMT3A R882H confers resistance to anthracycline-based chemotherapy by impairing chromatin remodeling at DNA damage sites. Hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) may partially overcome the epigenetic effects of DNMT3A loss, and venetoclax-based combinations show activity in DNMT3A-mutant AML.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / epigenetic driver, CHIP-associated

text_summary: TET2 loss-of-function mutations occur in approximately 10-20% of AML, 20-30% of myelodysplastic syndromes, and are one of the most common mutations in clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). TET2 encodes a methylcytosine dioxygenase that catalyzes the conversion of 5-methylcytosine to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine, an intermediate in active DNA demethylation. TET2 loss results in DNA hypermethylation and impaired hematopoietic stem cell differentiation. TET2 mutations are often early clonal events and can persist in remission as pre-leukemic clones. The prognostic impact of TET2 mutations in AML is context-dependent and modified by co-occurring mutations. Hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine) are commonly used in TET2-mutant myeloid neoplasms, and some retrospective studies suggest enhanced response to HMAs in TET2-mutant MDS/AML. Vitamin C has been investigated as an adjunct to enhance TET2 function in preclinical models.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / epigenetic driver, CHIP-associated, potential HMA sensitivity

text_summary: SPOP mutations occur in approximately 10-15% of primary prostate cancers, making SPOP the most commonly mutated gene in prostate cancer after TP53 and PTEN. Recurrent missense mutations cluster in the MATH substrate-binding domain (Y87C, F102C, F133V, W131G), impairing ubiquitin-mediated degradation of oncogenic substrates including AR coactivators, BET proteins, and ERG. SPOP-mutant prostate cancer defines a distinct molecular subtype that is mutually exclusive with ERG fusions and is associated with higher rates of CHD1 deletion and genomic instability. Emerging evidence suggests SPOP-mutant prostate cancers may be sensitive to BET inhibitors due to impaired BET protein degradation. SPOP mutations also predict sensitivity to abiraterone in some studies, and the prognostic significance is generally favorable compared to TP53/RB1-altered prostate cancer. SPOP mutation status is assessed by NGS-based comprehensive genomic profiling.
clinical_significance: Distinct molecular subtype / potential therapeutic implications

text_summary: Androgen receptor (AR) amplification is found in approximately 20-30% of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancers (mCRPC) and is a major mechanism of resistance to androgen deprivation therapy and second-generation AR-targeted agents. AR amplification increases AR protein expression, enabling continued signaling even at castrate levels of circulating androgens. AR amplification is frequently accompanied by other AR pathway alterations including AR mutations (L702H, T878A, H875Y) and AR-V7 splice variant expression, all of which contribute to castration resistance. Detection of AR amplification by ctDNA or tissue NGS predicts resistance to enzalutamide and abiraterone. Emerging therapeutic strategies include AR degraders (ARV-110/bavdegalutamide), bipolar androgen therapy, and combination approaches targeting downstream AR effectors. AR amplification assessment is increasingly incorporated into precision medicine panels for mCRPC treatment selection.
clinical_significance: Resistance to AR-targeted therapy / adverse prognosis in mCRPC

text_summary: RB1 loss through biallelic inactivation (deletion, mutation, or LOH) occurs in approximately 10-15% of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and is strongly associated with neuroendocrine differentiation, lineage plasticity, and aggressive clinical behavior. Combined RB1 and TP53 loss is the molecular hallmark of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (NEPC), a lethal variant that arises under selective pressure from AR-targeted therapy. RB1-deficient prostate cancers are resistant to enzalutamide and abiraterone but may respond to platinum-based chemotherapy, DLL3-targeted therapies (tarlatamab), and aurora kinase inhibitors (alisertib). RB1 loss also occurs at high frequency in small cell lung cancer (over 90%), retinoblastoma (bilateral germline), and osteosarcoma. CDK4/6 inhibitors are ineffective in RB1-null tumors due to the absence of the RB1 substrate. RB1 status assessment by IHC or NGS is critical for identifying lineage plasticity in prostate cancer.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / neuroendocrine transformation, AR therapy resistance

text_summary: The TMPRSS2-ERG fusion is the most common genomic rearrangement in prostate cancer, occurring in approximately 40-50% of cases. This fusion places the ERG transcription factor under androgen-responsive TMPRSS2 promoter control, resulting in androgen-driven ERG overexpression. ERG is an ETS family transcription factor that promotes invasion, angiogenesis, and epigenetic reprogramming when aberrantly expressed. TMPRSS2-ERG is detected by FISH, IHC (ERG protein), or RNA-based NGS. While TMPRSS2-ERG is not directly targetable with approved therapies, it has diagnostic utility (ERG IHC aids prostate cancer diagnosis on biopsy) and defines a molecular subtype mutually exclusive with SPOP mutations. ERG overexpression sensitizes prostate cancer cells to PARP inhibition in preclinical models, and clinical trials are evaluating PARP inhibitors in ERG-positive prostate cancer. The prognostic impact of TMPRSS2-ERG is generally neutral in the context of standard treatment.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic marker / defines molecular subtype

text_summary: CHEK2 germline pathogenic mutations, most commonly the 1100delC founder mutation, are found in approximately 1-3% of breast cancers and at lower frequencies in prostate, colorectal, and ovarian cancers. CHEK2 encodes checkpoint kinase 2, a serine/threonine kinase activated by ATM in response to DNA double-strand breaks that phosphorylates downstream targets including p53, BRCA1, and CDC25A to enforce cell cycle arrest and DNA repair. Heterozygous CHEK2 1100delC confers approximately 2-3 fold increased breast cancer risk, classifying it as a moderate-penetrance susceptibility gene. CHEK2-associated breast cancers tend to be ER-positive and may have distinct sensitivity profiles. While CHEK2-mutant cancers have impaired DNA damage response, clinical benefit from PARP inhibitors is uncertain and not as established as for BRCA1/2. NCCN guidelines recommend enhanced breast screening (annual MRI starting at age 40) for CHEK2 mutation carriers. CHEK2 is included in multigene hereditary cancer panels.
clinical_significance: Moderate-penetrance cancer susceptibility / enhanced screening recommended

text_summary: HRAS activating mutations (most commonly Q61R, G13R, and G12S) occur in approximately 5-8% of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas (HNSCC), 5-15% of bladder cancers, and in subsets of thyroid cancers and salivary gland tumors. Unlike KRAS and NRAS, HRAS mutations are relatively uncommon across most cancer types but are enriched in HNSCC, particularly in HPV-negative tumors. Tipifarnib, a farnesyltransferase inhibitor, targets HRAS by blocking its obligate farnesylation required for membrane localization and signaling activity. In the AIM-HN trial, tipifarnib demonstrated an ORR of 55% in HRAS-mutant HNSCC with variant allele frequency greater than 20%. Unlike KRAS, HRAS is uniquely dependent on farnesylation (not geranylgeranylation) for membrane association, making it susceptible to farnesyltransferase inhibition. HRAS mutation testing by NGS with VAF assessment is recommended for HNSCC patients being considered for tipifarnib.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to farnesyltransferase inhibition

text_summary: NOTCH1 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 10-15% of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas (HNSCC) and are also recurrent in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma and T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (activating mutations). In HNSCC, NOTCH1 acts as a tumor suppressor, and loss-of-function mutations disrupt terminal squamous differentiation. NOTCH1 mutations in HNSCC are associated with poor prognosis and frequently co-occur with TP53 mutations. Loss of NOTCH signaling leads to compensatory upregulation of WNT and Hedgehog pathways, providing potential therapeutic targets. NOTCH1-mutant HNSCC may respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors due to associated increased tumor mutational burden. In contrast, activating NOTCH1 mutations in T-ALL drive proliferation through MYC upregulation and are targeted by gamma-secretase inhibitors in clinical trials. NOTCH1 mutation status is assessed by comprehensive genomic profiling and may inform prognosis and immunotherapy selection in HNSCC.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / tumor suppressor loss, potential immunotherapy sensitivity

text_summary: FAT1 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 20-30% of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas, making it one of the most frequently mutated genes in HNSCC. FAT1 is a large protocadherin that functions as a tumor suppressor by inhibiting WNT signaling through binding to beta-catenin and promoting its membrane localization. Loss of FAT1 results in nuclear beta-catenin accumulation and activation of WNT target gene transcription, promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and tumor invasion. FAT1 mutations in HNSCC are associated with more aggressive clinical behavior and reduced overall survival. Therapeutic strategies targeting WNT pathway activation in FAT1-mutant tumors, including porcupine inhibitors and tankyrase inhibitors, are under preclinical and early clinical investigation. FAT1 mutations frequently co-occur with TP53, CDKN2A, and NOTCH1 alterations in HNSCC. FAT1 mutation status may inform risk stratification and eligibility for WNT-directed clinical trials.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / WNT pathway activation

text_summary: CASP8 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 8-10% of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas, particularly in HPV-negative tumors. CASP8 encodes caspase-8, the initiator caspase of the extrinsic apoptotic pathway activated by death receptors (FAS, TRAIL-R1/R2). Loss of CASP8 function renders tumor cells resistant to death-receptor-mediated apoptosis and may impair immune-mediated killing by cytotoxic T cells and NK cells. CASP8 mutations in HNSCC are associated with reduced response to radiation therapy and may confer resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors through impaired T-cell-mediated cytotoxicity. CASP8-mutant HNSCC frequently co-occurs with HRAS mutations, defining a distinct molecular subtype with active RAS signaling and apoptosis resistance. Emerging therapeutic strategies include IAP antagonists (SMAC mimetics) that can bypass caspase-8 deficiency by activating alternative cell death pathways. CASP8 mutation status is identified through comprehensive genomic profiling.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / apoptosis resistance

text_summary: PIK3CA E545K helical domain mutations occur in approximately 15-20% of HPV-positive head and neck squamous cell carcinomas, with lower frequency (6-8%) in HPV-negative HNSCC. In the HPV-positive HNSCC context, PIK3CA mutations are the most common co-occurring oncogenic alteration and represent a key node in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. Unlike HR-positive breast cancer where alpelisib is FDA-approved for PIK3CA mutations, there are no approved PI3K-targeted therapies specifically for PIK3CA-mutant HNSCC. However, PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib, copanlisib) and mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, temsirolimus) have shown clinical activity in HNSCC clinical trials. PIK3CA mutations in HPV-positive HNSCC may confer resistance to radiation therapy through enhanced DNA repair signaling. The combination of PI3K inhibitors with radiation or immunotherapy is under active investigation in PIK3CA-mutant HNSCC.
clinical_significance: Investigational / PI3K pathway activation, potential PI3K inhibitor target

text_summary: VHL (von Hippel-Lindau) biallelic inactivation through mutation and/or promoter methylation occurs in approximately 50-80% of clear cell renal cell carcinomas (ccRCC), making it the most common molecular alteration in this histology. VHL encodes the substrate recognition component of the E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that targets HIF-alpha transcription factors for proteasomal degradation under normoxic conditions. VHL loss stabilizes HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha, driving transcription of VEGF, PDGF, and other hypoxia-responsive genes that promote angiogenesis and tumor growth. This biology underlies the efficacy of anti-angiogenic therapy in ccRCC, including VEGFR TKIs (sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib, lenvatinib) and anti-VEGF antibodies (bevacizumab). Belzutifan, a first-in-class HIF-2alpha inhibitor, received FDA approval for VHL disease-associated RCC based on Study 004 demonstrating ORR of 49%. Germline VHL mutations cause VHL disease with predisposition to ccRCC, hemangioblastomas, and pheochromocytomas.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / sensitizing to anti-angiogenic and HIF-2alpha therapy

text_summary: PBRM1 truncating mutations occur in approximately 30-40% of clear cell renal cell carcinomas, making it the second most commonly mutated gene after VHL. PBRM1 encodes the BAF180 subunit of the PBAF chromatin remodeling complex, and its loss disrupts chromatin accessibility and gene expression regulation. PBRM1 loss has been associated with enhanced response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in retrospective analyses, including the CheckMate-025 and IMmotion150 trials, though this association is not consistently validated across studies. PBRM1-mutant ccRCC shows distinct transcriptomic profiles with increased angiogenic signaling and T-cell infiltration compared to PBRM1 wild-type tumors. PBRM1 mutations frequently co-occur with VHL loss and are generally associated with favorable or intermediate prognosis in ccRCC. PBRM1 mutation status is increasingly assessed by NGS panels and may inform treatment selection between immunotherapy and TKI-based regimens in ccRCC.
clinical_significance: Potential immunotherapy response biomarker in ccRCC

text_summary: SETD2 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 10-15% of clear cell renal cell carcinomas and are associated with aggressive clinical behavior and adverse prognosis. SETD2 is the sole histone methyltransferase responsible for H3K36 trimethylation (H3K36me3), a critical epigenetic mark involved in transcriptional elongation, RNA splicing, DNA damage repair, and DNA methylation. Loss of SETD2 function impairs mismatch repair and homologous recombination, contributing to microsatellite instability and genomic instability. SETD2-mutant ccRCC is associated with higher pathologic stage, grade, and reduced overall survival compared to SETD2 wild-type. Therapeutic strategies under investigation include EZH2 inhibitors (synthetic lethality from H3K36me3 loss and H3K27me3 redistribution), WEE1 inhibitors targeting DNA repair deficiency, and immune checkpoint inhibitors. SETD2 mutations also occur in hematologic malignancies (MDS, AML) and pediatric high-grade gliomas.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / epigenetic dysregulation

text_summary: BAP1 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 10-15% of clear cell renal cell carcinomas, 15-20% of mesotheliomas, and 40-50% of uveal melanomas. BAP1 encodes a nuclear deubiquitinase that removes monoubiquitin from histone H2A at lysine 119 (H2AK119Ub), counteracting Polycomb repressive complex 1 (PRC1) activity and regulating chromatin state. BAP1 loss in ccRCC is associated with high-grade tumors, rhabdoid features, and poor prognosis, in contrast to PBRM1 mutations which are associated with more favorable outcomes. BAP1 and PBRM1 mutations are largely mutually exclusive in ccRCC, defining distinct molecular subtypes. BAP1-mutant tumors may show vulnerability to EZH2 inhibition and HDAC inhibitors. Germline BAP1 mutations cause BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome with increased risk of uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, ccRCC, and cutaneous melanocytic tumors. BAP1 loss is detected by IHC (loss of nuclear staining) or NGS.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / tumor predisposition syndrome

text_summary: CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) activating mutations occur in approximately 25-30% of hepatocellular carcinomas, predominantly as missense mutations or in-frame deletions in exon 3 that eliminate GSK3-beta phosphorylation sites, stabilizing beta-catenin and activating WNT target gene transcription. CTNNB1-mutant HCC defines a distinct molecular subclass characterized by cholestatic features, lower AFP levels, chromosomal stability, and immune exclusion (cold tumor microenvironment). Importantly, CTNNB1-mutant HCC is associated with primary resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors, and retrospective analyses suggest reduced benefit from atezolizumab plus bevacizumab and nivolumab in this subgroup. WNT pathway activation through CTNNB1 mutation upregulates immune evasion mechanisms and reduces T-cell infiltration. Therapeutic strategies under investigation include CBP/beta-catenin inhibitors (PRI-724) and tankyrase inhibitors. CTNNB1 mutation status assessed by NGS may inform treatment selection in HCC, potentially favoring TKI-based regimens over immunotherapy.
drugs: sorafenib, lenvatinib (preferred over immunotherapy in CTNNB1-mutant)
clinical_significance: Resistance to immunotherapy / WNT pathway activation

text_summary: AXIN1 inactivating mutations occur in approximately 8-10% of hepatocellular carcinomas and result in WNT pathway activation through a distinct mechanism from CTNNB1 mutations. AXIN1 is a scaffolding protein in the beta-catenin destruction complex (alongside APC, GSK3-beta, and CK1), and its loss impairs beta-catenin phosphorylation and proteasomal degradation. Unlike CTNNB1-mutant HCC, AXIN1-mutant HCC is associated with a T-cell-inflamed tumor microenvironment and may respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors. AXIN1 and CTNNB1 mutations are largely mutually exclusive, and molecular subtyping that distinguishes these WNT pathway alterations may have treatment selection implications. AXIN1-mutant HCC tends to have higher AFP levels, more aggressive histologic features, and a proliferative gene expression signature. Therapeutic approaches targeting WNT signaling are under investigation, though clinical development of WNT pathway inhibitors has been challenging due to on-target toxicities.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / WNT pathway activation, potential immunotherapy sensitivity

text_summary: TSC1 (hamartin) and TSC2 (tuberin) inactivating mutations are found in approximately 5-8% of hepatocellular carcinomas and at higher frequencies in renal angiomyolipomas and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas associated with tuberous sclerosis complex. The TSC1-TSC2 complex acts as a GAP for the small GTPase Rheb, and its loss results in constitutive mTORC1 activation driving cell growth and proliferation. In HCC, TSC1/TSC2 loss defines a subset potentially sensitive to mTOR inhibitors. Everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, is FDA-approved for TSC-associated subependymal giant cell astrocytoma and renal angiomyolipoma. In the HCC context, everolimus showed limited benefit in unselected patients (EVOLVE-1 trial), but case reports and small series suggest enhanced activity in TSC1/TSC2-mutant HCC. Germline TSC1/TSC2 mutations cause tuberous sclerosis complex with multisystem hamartomas. Molecular profiling for TSC pathway alterations may identify HCC patients who benefit from mTOR-directed therapy.
clinical_significance: Potential mTOR inhibitor sensitivity / TSC-associated tumors

text_summary: POLE exonuclease domain mutations in endometrial cancer define the ultramutated molecular subtype per the TCGA classification, characterized by extraordinarily high tumor mutational burden (greater than 100 mutations per megabase) and a highly favorable prognosis with 5-year recurrence-free survival exceeding 95%. Hotspot mutations P286R and V411L account for the majority of pathogenic POLE variants in endometrial cancer. The hypermutation phenotype generates abundant neoantigens, driving robust anti-tumor immune responses with dense CD8+ T-cell infiltration. NCCN guidelines now incorporate POLE mutation status into risk stratification, allowing potential de-escalation of adjuvant therapy in early-stage endometrial cancer. POLE-ultramutated endometrial cancers respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors, and the KEYNOTE-158 trial demonstrated benefit of pembrolizumab in TMB-high tumors, which includes POLE-mutant cases. POLE mutation testing is recommended as part of the molecular classification of endometrial cancer alongside MMR/MSI, p53, and NSMP assessment.
clinical_significance: Highly favorable prognosis / immunotherapy responsive, adjuvant de-escalation

text_summary: ARID1A inactivating mutations occur in approximately 30-40% of endometrial cancers, with highest frequency in the endometrioid and clear cell histologic subtypes. ARID1A is a key subunit of the SWI/SNF (BAF) chromatin remodeling complex, and its loss disrupts chromatin accessibility at enhancer elements, leading to aberrant gene expression programs that promote tumor initiation and progression. ARID1A-mutant endometrial cancers frequently co-occur with PIK3CA mutations and microsatellite instability, and are often classified in the MSI-H or copy-number low (NSMP) molecular subtypes. ARID1A loss is associated with favorable response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in endometrial cancer, possibly due to increased interferon signaling and neoantigen presentation. Synthetic lethal therapeutic strategies targeting EZH2, ATR, ARID1B, and BRD2/4 are under clinical investigation. ARID1A protein loss detected by IHC correlates well with mutation status and serves as a practical screening tool.
clinical_significance: Potential immunotherapy biomarker / EZH2 synthetic lethality

text_summary: CTNNB1 exon 3 hotspot mutations occur in approximately 15-20% of endometrioid endometrial carcinomas, typically low-grade tumors classified in the no-specific-molecular-profile (NSMP) or copy-number-low subgroup per TCGA molecular classification. These mutations stabilize beta-catenin by preventing GSK3-beta-mediated phosphorylation and degradation, resulting in constitutive WNT pathway activation. CTNNB1-mutant endometrial cancers are associated with an intermediate-to-unfavorable prognosis compared to other NSMP tumors, with higher rates of recurrence in early-stage disease. The ProMisE and TransPORTEC molecular classifiers incorporate CTNNB1 mutation status to refine risk stratification within the NSMP subgroup, potentially identifying patients who benefit from adjuvant therapy that might otherwise be omitted. CTNNB1 nuclear immunoreactivity by IHC is a surrogate marker for activating mutations. WNT-directed therapeutic approaches are under investigation but no approved targeted therapies exist for this indication.
clinical_significance: Prognostic / intermediate-unfavorable within NSMP subtype

text_summary: Mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) in endometrial cancer occurs in approximately 25-30% of cases through somatic or germline inactivation of MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2, or through MLH1 promoter hypermethylation. dMMR endometrial cancer defines one of the four TCGA molecular subtypes and is associated with microsatellite instability, high tumor mutational burden, and immunogenic tumor microenvironment. Dostarlimab received FDA approval for dMMR recurrent or advanced endometrial cancer based on the GARNET trial, and the combination of pembrolizumab with lenvatinib is approved for advanced endometrial cancer regardless of MMR status (KEYNOTE-775 trial). The RUBY trial established dostarlimab plus carboplatin-paclitaxel as a first-line standard for dMMR advanced endometrial cancer with dramatic PFS improvement. Universal MMR/MSI testing by IHC for MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 is recommended for all endometrial cancers per NCCN guidelines, both for treatment selection and Lynch syndrome screening.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to immune checkpoint inhibitors

text_summary: KIT exon 11 mutations (in-frame deletions, point mutations, and insertions in the juxtamembrane domain) are the most common activating mutations in gastrointestinal stromal tumors, occurring in approximately 60-70% of all GIST. The juxtamembrane domain normally autoinhibits KIT kinase activity, and exon 11 mutations relieve this inhibition, resulting in constitutive receptor activation. KIT exon 11-mutant GIST is highly sensitive to imatinib, with response rates of 70-85% and median PFS of approximately 24 months in the advanced setting (B2222 and EORTC-ISG-AGITG trials).
