Adjuvant imatinib for three years significantly improves recurrence-free and overall survival in high-risk resected KIT exon 11-mutant GIST (SSGXVIII/AIO trial). Secondary resistance mutations typically arise in exon 13 (V654A), exon 14 (T670I gatekeeper), or exon 17 (D816V/H), guiding second-line therapy selection. Sunitinib and regorafenib are approved for imatinib-resistant GIST, and ripretinib provides broad KIT inhibition across resistance mutations.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to imatinib therapy

text_summary: PDGFRA D842V is an activation loop mutation found in approximately 5-6% of all GIST, primarily in gastric location with epithelioid morphology. This mutation confers primary resistance to imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib because these type II kinase inhibitors require the inactive DFG-out conformation that is precluded by the D842V substitution. Avapritinib, a type I kinase inhibitor designed to bind the active DFG-in conformation, received FDA approval specifically for PDGFRA D842V-mutant unresectable or metastatic GIST based on the NAVIGATOR trial, demonstrating ORR of 88% with complete responses in 13% of patients. Avapritinib has transformed the treatment of this previously imatinib-refractory GIST subtype. PDGFRA exon 18 D842V testing by allele-specific PCR or NGS is essential in KIT wild-type GIST. NCCN guidelines recommend avapritinib as the preferred first-line therapy for PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST rather than imatinib.
clinical_significance: Imatinib-resistant / sensitizing to avapritinib

text_summary: ERBB2 (HER2) activating mutations, most commonly exon 20 insertions (A775_G776insYVMA), occur in approximately 2-4% of NSCLCs, predominantly in never-smokers and adenocarcinoma histology. Unlike HER2 amplification in breast cancer, HER2-mutant NSCLC is driven by kinase domain mutations that constitutively activate HER2 signaling. Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) received FDA approval for HER2-mutant metastatic NSCLC based on the DESTINY-Lung02 trial, demonstrating ORR of 49% and median PFS of 9.9 months. This represented a breakthrough for HER2-mutant NSCLC, which previously lacked effective targeted therapy. Traditional HER2-directed agents (trastuzumab, pertuzumab) showed limited single-agent activity in HER2-mutant NSCLC. Poziotinib and pyrotinib, designed to target exon 20 insertions, have shown activity in clinical trials. HER2 mutation testing by NGS is recommended for all advanced NSCLC patients per NCCN guidelines as part of comprehensive biomarker assessment.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to trastuzumab deruxtecan

text_summary: MYC amplification is a recurrent oncogenic alteration found across multiple cancer types, including approximately 15-20% of breast cancers (enriched in triple-negative and HER2-positive subtypes), 10-15% of ovarian cancers, 5-10% of NSCLCs, and at high frequency in Burkitt lymphoma (MYC translocation) and medulloblastoma. MYC encodes a master transcription factor that regulates cell proliferation, metabolism, and apoptosis, and its amplification drives aggressive tumor biology with high proliferative indices. MYC is considered one of the most challenging oncogenic targets due to the lack of enzymatic activity or accessible binding pockets. However, indirect MYC-targeting strategies under clinical investigation include BET bromodomain inhibitors (which suppress MYC transcription), CDK7/CDK9 inhibitors (targeting transcriptional machinery), AURKA inhibitors (disrupting MYC protein stabilization), and synthetic lethal approaches. MYC amplification is associated with adverse prognosis and chemotherapy resistance across tumor types.
clinical_significance: Adverse prognosis / investigational indirect MYC-targeting strategies

text_summary: CCNE1 (cyclin E1) amplification occurs in approximately 15-20% of high-grade serous ovarian cancers and defines a molecular subtype associated with primary platinum resistance and poor prognosis. CCNE1 amplification is mutually exclusive with BRCA1/2 mutations and homologous recombination deficiency, placing these tumors in the HR-proficient, copy-number-high molecular subgroup. Overexpression of cyclin E1 drives uncontrolled CDK2 activation, resulting in replication stress, chromosomal instability, and S-phase checkpoint override. CCNE1-amplified ovarian cancers are resistant to PARP inhibitors due to intact homologous recombination. Therapeutic strategies under investigation include CDK2 inhibitors (which directly target the CCNE1 effector kinase), WEE1 inhibitors (adavosertib, which exploits replication stress), and ATR inhibitors. CCNE1 amplification status identified by NGS or FISH may guide treatment away from PARP inhibitors and toward replication-stress-targeting agents in ovarian cancer.
clinical_significance: Platinum/PARP inhibitor resistance / replication stress vulnerability

text_summary: NRG1 (neuregulin 1) fusions are rare oncogenic drivers found in approximately 0.2% of solid tumors across multiple histologies, with relative enrichment in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung (1-7%), pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (0.5%), and gallbladder cancer. Multiple fusion partners have been identified (CD74, ATP1B1, SDC4, SLC3A2, VAMP2), all of which retain the NRG1 EGF-like domain that activates HER2-HER3 heterodimer signaling. Zenocutuzumab, a bispecific antibody that blocks NRG1-mediated HER2-HER3 dimerization, received FDA accelerated approval for NRG1 fusion-positive NSCLC, pancreatic, and other solid tumors based on the eNRGy trial. Afatinib, a pan-HER inhibitor, has also shown clinical activity in case reports. NRG1 fusions are detected by RNA-based NGS and are typically mutually exclusive with other oncogenic drivers. Testing is recommended in driver-negative cancers, particularly invasive mucinous lung adenocarcinoma.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to HER2-HER3 blockade (tissue-agnostic)

text_summary: PALB2 pathogenic mutations in pancreatic cancer occur in approximately 3-4% of cases and represent the third most common HRD gene alteration after BRCA2 and BRCA1 in this histology. PALB2 bridges BRCA1 and BRCA2 in the homologous recombination repair complex, and its biallelic loss creates HR deficiency analogous to BRCA mutations. PALB2-mutant pancreatic cancers show sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX, gemcitabine-cisplatin) and PARP inhibitors. The POLO trial established olaparib maintenance in BRCA-mutant pancreatic cancer, and PALB2-mutant cases are increasingly included in expanded PARP inhibitor eligibility based on the biological rationale of shared HR deficiency. Germline PALB2 mutations confer approximately 2-3 fold increased lifetime risk of pancreatic cancer, and NCCN guidelines recommend pancreatic cancer screening with MRI or EUS for PALB2 carriers with family history. PALB2 is included in multigene panels for hereditary pancreatic cancer risk assessment.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / PARP inhibitor and platinum sensitivity

text_summary: ATM inactivating mutations occur in approximately 5-8% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas, making ATM one of the most commonly altered DNA damage response genes in this cancer type. ATM is a master kinase of the DNA damage response that phosphorylates over 700 substrates following DNA double-strand breaks, coordinating cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, and apoptosis. Biallelic ATM loss impairs homologous recombination and may create a BRCAness phenotype with potential sensitivity to PARP inhibitors and platinum chemotherapy. However, clinical evidence for PARP inhibitor benefit in ATM-mutant cancers is less consistent than for BRCA-mutant cancers, as demonstrated in the PROfound trial where ATM-mutant prostate cancer showed attenuated olaparib benefit. ATR inhibitors (ceralasertib, berzosertib) exploit the synthetic lethal relationship between ATM loss and ATR inhibition and are being investigated in ATM-deficient solid tumors. ATM germline mutations confer moderate increased risk of pancreatic and breast cancer.
clinical_significance: Potential DDR-targeted therapy sensitivity / uncertain PARP inhibitor benefit

text_summary: RAD51C pathogenic mutations in ovarian cancer occur in approximately 1-2% of high-grade serous ovarian carcinomas and contribute to homologous recombination deficiency. RAD51C is essential for RAD51 filament formation and Holliday junction resolution during homologous recombination DNA repair. Biallelic RAD51C inactivation creates an HRD phenotype functionally similar to BRCA1/2 loss, with sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors. RAD51C-mutant ovarian cancers were included in the HRD-positive cohorts of the ARIEL3 (rucaparib maintenance) and PRIMA (niraparib maintenance) trials, which demonstrated PFS benefit for HRD-positive patients. Germline RAD51C mutations confer an estimated 5-10% lifetime risk of ovarian cancer, and NCCN guidelines recommend risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy for carriers, typically between ages 45-50. PARP inhibitor resistance through RAD51C reversion mutations restoring the open reading frame has been documented, paralleling the BRCA reversion resistance mechanism.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / PARP inhibitor and platinum sensitivity, cancer predisposition

text_summary: ARID1A inactivating mutations are the hallmark molecular alteration of ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), occurring in approximately 40-57% of cases. OCCC is a chemoresistant ovarian cancer subtype with poor response to platinum-based chemotherapy (ORR 20-30% compared to 70-80% in high-grade serous). ARID1A loss in OCCC disrupts SWI/SNF-mediated chromatin remodeling, creating dependency on EZH2 methyltransferase activity, and EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat) show preclinical synthetic lethality in ARID1A-deficient models. ARID1A-mutant OCCC also shows vulnerability to ATR inhibitors (ceralasertib) and PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors, as ARID1A loss frequently co-occurs with PIK3CA activating mutations in OCCC. Immune checkpoint inhibitors have shown modest activity in OCCC, with ARID1A-mutant cases potentially showing enhanced benefit through increased tumor immunogenicity. Clinical trials specifically enrolling ARID1A-mutant cancers are ongoing, and ARID1A protein loss by IHC is a reliable surrogate for mutation status.
clinical_significance: Platinum resistance / EZH2 and ATR synthetic lethality

text_summary: SMARCB1 (INI1/SNF5/BAF47) biallelic loss is the defining molecular alteration of epithelioid sarcoma, present in approximately 90% of cases. Epithelioid sarcoma is a rare soft tissue sarcoma that predominantly affects young adults and has limited response to conventional chemotherapy. SMARCB1 is a core subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, and its loss creates epigenetic dependency on the PRC2 complex, specifically EZH2 methyltransferase activity. Tazemetostat, a selective EZH2 inhibitor, received FDA approval for metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma not eligible for curative surgery based on a phase 2 trial demonstrating ORR of 15% with durable responses (median DOR not reached). SMARCB1 loss is diagnosed by IHC showing complete loss of INI1 nuclear expression, which is both diagnostic and predictive of tazemetostat eligibility. SMARCB1 loss also defines malignant rhabdoid tumors, atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumors, and renal medullary carcinoma.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic / sensitizing to EZH2 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: IDH1 R132C is the second most common IDH1 mutation in AML (after R132H) and is the predominant IDH1 mutation in intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma and chondrosarcoma. This missense mutation substitutes arginine with cysteine at position 132 in the active site, creating neomorphic enzyme activity that converts alpha-ketoglutarate to the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). Accumulation of 2-HG competitively inhibits alpha-KG-dependent dioxygenases including TET2 and histone demethylases, causing DNA and histone hypermethylation that blocks cellular differentiation. Ivosidenib is FDA-approved for IDH1-mutant relapsed/refractory AML and IDH1-mutant previously treated cholangiocarcinoma (ClarIDHy trial: PFS 2.7 vs 1.4 months, OS benefit after crossover adjustment). IDH1 R132C responds to ivosidenib comparably to R132H. IDH1 mutation testing by NGS or allele-specific PCR is recommended at diagnosis in AML and in cholangiocarcinoma for treatment selection.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to IDH1 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: IDH2 R172K is an IDH2 hotspot mutation found in approximately 1-3% of AML cases, occurring at a lower frequency than IDH2 R140Q but with distinct clinical and biological features. IDH2 R172K produces higher levels of D-2-hydroxyglutarate compared to R140Q and is associated with a unique hypermethylation pattern. In AML, IDH2 R172K is mutually exclusive with NPM1 mutations and tends to occur in the absence of other common co-occurring mutations, defining a molecularly distinct AML subset. Enasidenib, an oral IDH2 inhibitor, is FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory IDH2-mutant AML and is active against both R140Q and R172K variants. The overall response rate to enasidenib in the AG221-C-001 trial was 40.3%, with responses observed across both IDH2 hotspot mutations. Differentiation syndrome is a class-effect toxicity requiring vigilant monitoring. IDH2 R172K also occurs in gliomas and is associated with favorable prognosis in that context.
clinical_significance: Sensitizing / response to IDH2 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: NRAS Q61R is the most common NRAS hotspot mutation, found in approximately 15-20% of melanomas, 5-10% of AML, and 3-5% of colorectal cancers. This gain-of-function mutation locks NRAS in the GTP-bound active state by impairing intrinsic GTPase activity, resulting in constitutive activation of RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT signaling cascades. In melanoma, NRAS Q61R is mutually exclusive with BRAF V600E and defines a distinct melanoma subtype with aggressive biology. MEK inhibitors (binimetinib) showed modest single-agent activity in NRAS-mutant melanoma in the NEMO trial (PFS 2.8 vs 1.5 months versus dacarbazine). Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab+nivolumab) are the preferred systemic therapy for NRAS-mutant melanoma. Direct NRAS inhibitors are not clinically available due to the smooth GTPase surface lacking drug-binding pockets. Combination strategies targeting downstream MAPK signaling are under active investigation in NRAS-mutant cancers.
clinical_significance: MEK inhibitor sensitivity / immunotherapy preferred
